draft.introduction: The Gospel of Mary survives in two fragmentary Greek papyri (third century) and a longer Coptic translation in the Berlin Codex (fifth century). Roughly half the text is missing. What remains is among the most striking documents in the Gnostic corpus: after the risen Jesus departs, the male disciples are afraid and despairing, and it is Mary Magdalene who comforts them and reveals a private teaching she received from the Saviour in a vision. Pages 7 through 10 contain the heart of the text: Mary’s account of the soul’s ascent past the cosmic powers. The soul encounters four powers—Darkness, Desire, Ignorance, and Wrath—and must defeat each one through knowledge of its true nature. This is theosis as escape: the soul recognizes that it does not belong to the material world, that the powers have no legitimate authority over it, and that its true home is in the silence of the divine. The passage is also remarkable for what follows it—Peter’s challenge to Mary’s authority, and Levi’s rebuke of Peter—which makes this text central to debates about gender, authority, and whose experience of the divine counts.

translation.text: Mary said: “What is hidden from you I will reveal to you.” And she began to speak these words to them. She said: “I saw the Lord in a vision, and I said to him, ‘Lord, I saw you today in a vision.’ He answered and said to me, ‘Blessed are you, since you did not waver at the sight of me. For where the mind is, there is the treasure.’”
translation.original_text: ⲙⲁⲣⲓⲁ ⲇⲉ ⲓⲉⲵⲁⲥ ⲵⲉ ⲓⲉⲧⲑⲓⲓ ⲉⲧⲃⲉⲓⲓ ⲉⲣⲕⲥ

translation.text: “The soul answered the first power, Darkness, and said: ‘What binds me has been slain, and what surrounds me has been destroyed, and my desire has been brought to an end, and ignorance has died. In a world I was set free from a world, and in a type from a heavenly type, and from the fetter of forgetfulness which is not eternal. From this hour on, for the time of the moment, I will receive rest in silence.’”

The soul then came to the second power, which is called Desire. The power said: “I did not see you going down, but now I see you going up. Why do you lie, since you belong to me?” The soul answered and said: “I saw you, but you did not see me, nor did you know me. I was with you as with a garment, and you did not recognize me.”
translation.original_text: ⲧⲻⲥⲑ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲩⲉⲓ ⲉⲧⲙⲉⲑⲙⲁⲑ ⲇⲥⲁⲙⲓⲩ ⲉⲧⲉⲧⲑⲧⲑⲙⲓⲁ

translation.text: The soul came to the third power, which is called Ignorance. The power questioned the soul, saying: “Where are you going? You are bound by wickedness. You are bound, so do not judge!” The soul said: “Why do you judge me, although I have not judged? I was bound, though I have not bound. I was not recognized, but I have recognized that all things are being dissolved, both the earthly and the heavenly.”

The soul overcame the third power and ascended, and saw the fourth power. It had seven forms: the first form is Darkness; the second, Desire; the third, Ignorance; the fourth, Zeal for Death; the fifth, the Kingdom of the Flesh; the sixth, the Foolish Wisdom of the Flesh; the seventh, the Wrathful Wisdom. These are the seven powers of Wrath.

They questioned the soul: “Where do you come from, slayer of humans? Where are you going, destroyer of spaces?” The soul answered and said: “What binds me has been slain, and what surrounds me has been overcome, and my desire has been ended, and ignorance has died. In a world I was released from a world, and in a type from a heavenly type. The fetter of forgetfulness is temporary. From this hour on, I will attain rest in the silence, having received rest in the time of the moment.”
translation.original_text: ⲁⲥⲣⲕⲧⲕⲥ ⲇⲉ ⲣⲉⲧⲻⲥⲑ ⲵⲉ ⲓⲉⲧⲉⲣⲩⲥ ⲉⲧⲃⲉ ⲣⲉⲧⲑⲕⲩ ⲣⲉⲧⲣⲉⲥⲁⲓⲁⲥⲩⲓⲩ

translator_notes.note: The saying “Where the mind is, there is the treasure” inverts Matthew 6:21 (“Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also”). In the Gnostic reading, the mind (nous) itself is the treasure—the divine faculty within the human being that enables direct contact with God.

The soul’s declaration “In a world I was set free from a world, and in a type from a heavenly type” is one of the most compressed statements of Gnostic soteriology in existence. It suggests that liberation happens simultaneously on multiple levels: freedom from the material world and from the cosmic archetypes that structure it.

The seven forms of the fourth power correspond loosely to the seven planets of ancient cosmology, which in Gnostic thought are the seven archons who imprison the soul. The soul must pass through all seven to return to the Pleroma. This schema appears in many Gnostic texts, most notably in the Apocryphon of John.
